title: New York Fed Releases New Weekly Report on Oil Prices

source.name: Federal Reserve Bank of New York

summary: The Federal Reserve Bank of New York announced the launch of a new weekly Oil Price Dynamics Report on May 2, 2016. The report uses statistical modeling to decompose oil price changes into demand and supply shocks. The launch announcement notes that oil prices had risen recently as demand expectations improved and supply conditions tightened, recovering to levels seen at the start of October 2015.
key_points: New York Fed launched the Oil Price Dynamics Report, a weekly analytical tool released each Monday at 3:00 p.m.
The report uses a statistical model with financial variables to separate demand-driven from supply-driven oil price changes.
The report provides cumulative percentage differences since the beginning of the previous quarter plus longer-term oil price views.
Supply-driven oil price weakness occurred in Q4 2015, following sustained weakness over that quarter.
Q2-Q3 2015 oil price declines were influenced more by global demand expectations than supply factors.
The analysis distinguishes between demand and supply shocks, which have different implications for the U.S. economic outlook.
why_it_matters: This announcement introduces a new analytical tool for decomposing oil price movements into structural components. Understanding whether oil price changes stem from demand or supply factors helps economists and policymakers assess the likely persistence and macroeconomic impact of price movements. However, this is only an announcement about the tool's launch, not the tool's actual analytical output.
macro_context: The source explains that demand shocks and supply shocks have different implications for the U.S. economic outlook. Demand-driven oil price increases may signal stronger global growth, while supply-driven increases may be more inflationary without the growth benefit. The 2015 context showed a shift from supply-driven weakness (2012-2014 period) to demand-driven factors.
